critique.summary: The article describes the author's experience running a systems reading group at Microsoft since 2021, initially focused on database internals and later expanding to broader systems topics. The group evolved from informal paper discussions to guided reading series, fostering learning and cross-team connections among participants.
mainPoints: The reading group started in 2021 focused on database internals and expanded over time to cover broader systems topics.
The format evolved from one-off paper discussions to guided reading series for deeper understanding.
The group encourages curiosity-driven exploration beyond databases, including memory hierarchies, consensus protocols, and datacenter foundations.
Key lessons include starting small, maintaining consistency, allowing organic scope growth, having co-organizers, and making participation easy even without preparation.
The group has provided both learning opportunities and valuable professional connections within Microsoft.
critique.truthfulness: The article is a personal account by a Microsoft employee about their experience running an internal reading group. The claims about the group's topics, format, and evolution are consistent with typical practices in technical reading groups and are plausible. The references to well-known papers and books in the systems and database fields (e.g., Paxos Made Simple, the Red Book) are accurate. The article does not make extraordinary or controversial claims and primarily shares subjective experiences and advice, which are inherently truthful from the author's perspective.
considerations: The article does not provide external verification or perspectives from other participants or Microsoft as an organization.
It does not discuss challenges or limitations faced by the reading group beyond occasional quiet periods.
The impact of the reading group on participants' work or career development is anecdotal and not quantified.
The article focuses on positive aspects and may omit any negative experiences or conflicts.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around experiences and challenges of running engineering or systems reading groups in various organizations. Participants share insights on maintaining engagement, the value of such groups, and practical tips for success.

comments.author: rectang
comments.text:
I ran reading groups over several years a medium-sized startup I worked for and for an open source community I was a part of. The groups were targeted not only at engineering staff but also semi-technical positions such as product management and low-skilled data pipeline specialists, with the aim of building bridges between departments and internal recruitment.

Running those reading groups was basically how I acquired a lot of knowledge I didn't get because I didn't major in Comp Sci as an undergrad.

Books/MOOCs we went through:

* Pro Git (Chacon) [three times]

* Programming Language Pragmatics (Scott)

* Calculus first semester (Coursera/OSU/Fowler)

* Eloquent JavaScript (Haverbeke)

* Learning Perl (Schwartz/Foy) [two or three times]

* Coding the Matrix (Coursera/Brown/Klein) [didn't finish]

* Object-Oriented Programming: An Evolutionary Approach (Cox)

* Learning Core Audio (Adamson/Avila)

The level of commitment from the participants was mixed. Nobody came for the free lunch (although arranging free lunch was essential in getting people to show up). Many people had ambitions that outstripped their commitment. But there were plenty of people who took advantage and learned tons.

One fellow, who I feel immensely fortunate to have known, went from zero experience to arguably our most productive IC within 2 years, and eventually landed at a FAANG. He also took a turn leading the discussion group.

Another participant was a Product Manager who became much better able to communicate with Engineering after improving her understanding of programming fundamentals.

The sessions were generally organized around questions that people would bring about the material — my motto was "we'll struggle through together". I preferred questions posed by others but always had enough of my own to fill space.

The tips I would have for people running such groups:

First you need a discussion leader. You need someone who can get the group unstuck and who knows the material. It's the same dynamic as having a good TA for a college discussion section.

Second, remove all friction. Corporate won't buy books? Screw it, I bought 'em myself. Corporate won't arrange for lunch? Screw it, I bought it for everybody. (Our CTO was highly supportive but once the company got acquired we couldn't get budget approved anymore). The total outlay I made leading the group was a few thousand dollars — far less than I would have paid for formal courses where I would have learned less.
comments.analysis: This comment provides detailed, practical advice based on extensive personal experience, making it highly credible and constructive.

comments.author: mohitk05
comments.text:
Congratulations on the five year mark! I co-run a similar paper reading group at Zalando (European e-commerce) and recently shared our learnings/experience[1] of running such a group for over a year and I'm happy to see so many similarities.

We focus our papers around distributed systems, software engineering and languages and try to cover more ground on the applications of the concepts discussed in the papers. The blog post also contains a "blueprint" for someone who is looking to start a similar group.

What has driven the meetups throughout the year is we being two organisers, driving the topic ourselves by preparing a presentation (not everyone would have the time to pre-read) and making the format conversational/open - very similar to what Armaan (op) shared.

One of our recent experiments was doing a collab with the Berlin Systems Group[2] where we organised an external-facing event in Berlin with attendees from outside our organisation. I was so happy to see a nice small group turn up for the event and thoroughly enjoy it!

[1] https://engineering.zalando.com/posts/2026/01/running-an-eng... [2] https://luma.com/y9edbih7
comments.analysis: This comment shares relevant experience and successful strategies, including collaboration and presentation-driven formats, supporting the main discussion well.

comments.author: Foe
comments.text: Hi HN, I've been organizing a systems reading group at Microsoft for five years now. I wrote down some takeaways on what worked (and what didn't). I'd love to hear if anyone else has successfully kept an engineering reading group alive at their company, or if you have any favorite systems papers we should add to our list!
comments.analysis: As the original poster, this comment sets the context and invites further discussion, showing openness and a constructive approach.

comments.author: oa335
comments.text:
I would be interested to hear others experiences with running these types of groups. We’ve tried this a couple of times at my current job and both times it’s petered out - people don’t do the assigned reading and then just stop attending.

Any suggestions on how to keep such a group alive?
comments.analysis: This comment raises a common challenge in sustaining reading groups, contributing a realistic perspective and seeking advice.

comments.author: smokel
comments.text: I understand that in a research lab or in academia, this is common practice.  But in the more menial coding industry that most of us are probably in, how do you find time for this?  Do people read papers in their spare time and discuss over lunch, or are there enlightened managers who support this during working hours?
comments.analysis: This comment questions the feasibility of such groups in typical industry settings, highlighting practical concerns about time and management support.

comments.author: RomanPushkin
comments.text: I've seen such reading groups. I worked for Atlassian, and there was a reading group. My impression is it was organized only as a low effort just to demonstrate that IC is going extra mile for the company. The quality of such reading groups were quite low. And it was expected that you would attend this reading group at a lunch time. You're taking your lunch with you, and instead of enjoying your meal, you're cramped in a small room with coworkers who also got their sandwiches and sugar soda. Horrible experience, and zero value.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a negative personal experience, which may be valid but is anecdotal and less constructive compared to others.

comments.author: mememememememo
comments.text:
I am so jealous. 25 years of crud and framework chasing and I'd never get close to that. I am slightly closer now but not much.

If I had my time again I'd honestly just apply for job 1 make ends meet then continously apply to get a job like this where you can go deep.

Money is probably good but just for the interestingness. That DCOM and SOAP shit I did is worthless now. Most tech is non compounding.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses frustration and envy, reflecting a personal viewpoint rather than contributing directly to the discussion about reading groups.
